---
title: "Health Insurance & Mental Health"
subtitle: "Navigating coverage, mental health support, and wellbeing as a PhD student"
description: "Insurance glossary, coverage-gap solutions, mental health resource tiers, US crisis hotlines, and advisor-relationship red flags for PhD students."
order: 4
---

Access to affordable, comprehensive health insurance and mental health support is a critical — and often underdiscussed — dimension of graduate student well-being. This guide covers understanding your university health plan, supplementing coverage gaps, navigating mental health resources, and recognizing and responding to crisis situations.

---

## 1. Health Insurance: Key Questions

- [ ] Is health insurance **provided** by the university or do I need to enroll separately?
- [ ] What is the **annual premium** and what portion (if any) do I pay?
- [ ] What is the **deductible** (amount I pay before insurance kicks in)?
- [ ] What is the **out-of-pocket maximum** (the most I could ever pay in a year)?
- [ ] Is my primary care physician (PCP) and any existing specialists **in-network**?
- [ ] Does the plan cover **mental health and substance use treatment** at the same level as physical health? (Required by the Mental Health Parity Act.)
- [ ] What are the **dental and vision** benefits? Are they included or purchased separately?
- [ ] Does the plan cover **preventive care** (annual physicals, vaccines, screenings) at 100%?
- [ ] Does the plan cover **reproductive health** (contraception, prenatal care, fertility, abortion services)?
- [ ] Is there **24/7 telehealth access** included?

---

## 2. Understanding Your Health Plan

### Key Terms Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Premium** | The monthly cost of the plan, regardless of whether you use healthcare |
| **Deductible** | Amount you pay out-of-pocket before insurance begins paying for covered services |
| **Copay** | A fixed fee you pay at each visit (e.g., $20 for a PCP visit) |
| **Coinsurance** | The percentage you pay after meeting your deductible (e.g., 20%) |
| **Out-of-Pocket Maximum** | The most you will pay in a plan year; after this, insurance pays 100% |
| **In-Network** | Providers who have a contract with your insurer (lower cost to you) |
| **Out-of-Network** | Providers without a contract (much higher cost, often not covered) |
| **FSA** | Flexible Spending Account — pre-tax money for medical expenses (use it or lose it) |
| **HSA** | Health Savings Account — pre-tax savings for medical expenses; rolls over year to year (requires a High Deductible Health Plan) |
| **EOB** | Explanation of Benefits — a statement from your insurer showing what was billed, paid, and owed |

### Annual Health Insurance Checklist

- [ ] Review the **Summary of Benefits and Coverage (SBC)** — a standardized 2-page summary required by law.
- [ ] Confirm your **enrollment window** — graduate student plans often renew each August/September.
- [ ] If you are under 26, compare the cost of staying on a **parent's plan** vs. the university plan.
- [ ] If you are married/partnered, compare **individual vs. family plan** costs.
- [ ] Check whether the plan includes **dental and vision**, or whether you need to enroll separately.
- [ ] Set up an **HSA or FSA** if eligible — reduces taxable income.

---

## 3. Common Coverage Gaps and How to Address Them

| Gap | Solution |
|---|---|
| High deductible (>$1,500/individual) | Enroll in an HSA; contribute the maximum to offset risk |
| Dental coverage missing or weak | Look into standalone dental insurance (e.g., Delta Dental individual plans, ~$20–$40/mo) or a dental school clinic for low-cost care |
| Vision coverage missing | VSP individual plans start at ~$13/mo; many optometrists accept direct-pay patients at $50–$100 for an exam |
| Mental health visits limited or high copay | Use university counseling center (often free); ask HR about mental health parity complaint process |
| Prescription costs high | Use [GoodRx](https://www.goodrx.com/) for discounts; ask your doctor for generic equivalents |
| Reproductive health not covered | Contact your Title IX office; [Planned Parenthood](https://www.plannedparenthood.org/) offers sliding-scale services |
| Telehealth not included | [MDLive](https://www.mdlive.com/), [Teladoc](https://www.teladoc.com/), and similar services offer low-cost per-visit access |

---

## 4. Mental Health Resources

### Why Graduate Students Are at High Risk

Research consistently shows PhD students experience depression and anxiety at rates 6 times higher than the general population (Evans et al., *Nature Biotechnology*, 2018). Stressors include:

- Funding uncertainty
- Isolation and lack of social support
- Advisor relationship difficulties
- Imposter syndrome
- Career uncertainty
- Long work hours with poor work-life boundaries

### Recognizing When to Seek Help

It is appropriate to seek support if you experience, for more than two weeks:

- [ ] Persistent sadness, emptiness, or hopelessness
- [ ] Loss of interest in research or activities you previously enjoyed
- [ ] Significant changes in sleep, appetite, or weight
- [ ] Difficulty concentrating or making decisions
- [ ] Feelings of worthlessness or excessive guilt
- [ ] Thoughts of death or suicide *(if present, seek help immediately)*

### Levels of Mental Health Support

| Level | What It Is | How to Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Self-help / peer support** | Apps, journaling, graduate student mental health groups | Free; see apps below |
| **University counseling center** | Free individual and group therapy for enrolled students | Campus counseling center website; typically limited to 6–12 sessions |
| **University psychiatry** | Medication management and psychiatric evaluation | Referral from counseling center |
| **Community mental health** | Sliding-scale therapy in the community | [Psychology Today therapist finder](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapists) |
| **In-network therapist (via insurance)** | Covered (partially) by health plan | Check insurer's provider directory |
| **Crisis services** | Immediate help for safety crises | See Crisis Resources below |

### Crisis Resources (US)

| Resource | Contact | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline** | Call or text **988** | 24/7; free; confidential |
| **Crisis Text Line** | Text **HOME** to **741741** | 24/7; text-based |
| **SAMHSA National Helpline** | 1-800-662-4357 | 24/7; substance use and mental health |
| **Trans Lifeline** | 877-565-8860 | By and for trans people |
| **Trevor Project (LGBTQ+ youth)** | 1-866-488-7386 or text START to 678-678 | 24/7 |
| **Campus Emergency / Security** | Your campus emergency number | Physical safety crises |

> **If you are in immediate danger, call 911 or go to your nearest emergency room.**

---

## 5. Mental Health Apps and Self-Help Tools

| App / Resource | Focus | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| [Headspace](https://www.headspace.com/) | Meditation, stress reduction | Free trial; many universities offer free access |
| [Calm](https://www.calm.com/) | Mindfulness, sleep | Free trial; discounts available |
| [Woebot](https://woebothealth.com/) | CBT-based mental health chatbot | Free |
| [Sanvello](https://www.sanvello.com/) | Anxiety, depression self-management | Free basic; covered by some insurance |
| [7 Cups](https://www.7cups.com/) | Peer support chat | Free |
| [MindShift CBT](https://www.anxietycanada.com/resources/mindshift-cbt/) | Anxiety management | Free |

---

## 6. Advisor Relationship and Mental Health

The advisor relationship is one of the strongest predictors of graduate student mental health. Signs of an unhealthy advising relationship include:

- Unpredictable or volatile emotional reactions
- Unrealistic demands on time (including nights, weekends, holidays)
- Dismissiveness about mental health or personal circumstances
- Taking credit for student work or withholding authorship
- Isolation from other mentors or collaborators

**If you are experiencing difficulties:**

1. Consult your program's **graduate student ombudsperson** or **Dean of Graduate Studies** — these conversations are typically confidential.
2. Reach out to a **committee member** or secondary mentor.
3. Document interactions in writing where possible.
4. Know that **changing advisors is possible** at most institutions, even if it feels difficult.

---

## 7. Physical Health and Wellness

Don't overlook the basics — they significantly affect mental health:

- [ ] Schedule an **annual physical** and use your preventive care benefits.
- [ ] Use the **campus recreation center** (typically free or very low-cost for students).
- [ ] Prioritize **7–9 hours of sleep** — chronic sleep deprivation impairs cognition, emotional regulation, and immune function.
- [ ] Set **boundaries around work hours** — research shows diminishing returns beyond 50 hours/week.
- [ ] Maintain **social connections** outside your lab; isolation is a major risk factor for depression.

---

## 8. Additional Resources

- [Graduate Mental Health Toolkit (NAGPS)](https://nagps.org/)
- [Active Minds](https://www.activeminds.org/) — mental health advocacy in higher education
- [JED Foundation](https://jedfoundation.org/) — emotional health and suicide prevention in teens/young adults
- [NAMI (National Alliance on Mental Illness)](https://www.nami.org/) — helpline: 1-800-950-6264
- [SAMHSA Treatment Locator](https://findtreatment.gov/)
- Evans, T.M. et al. (2018). Evidence for a mental health crisis in graduate education. *Nature Biotechnology*, 36, 282–284. [https://doi.org/10.1038/nbt.4089](https://doi.org/10.1038/nbt.4089)

---

*Last reviewed: 2024. Mental health resources, hotline numbers, and insurance requirements can change. Verify current resources at your institution's counseling center website.*
